journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America
title: NAT10/ac4C drives intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma by suppressing transposable elements via chromatin remodeling
abstract: Intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma (ICC) poses a significant clinical challenge due to its insidious onset, aggressive biological behavior, and propensity for early metastasis, contributing to a dismal 5-y survival rate of less than 10%. Despite advances in understanding the dynamic changes in gene regulatory networks and chromatin landscapes during tumorigenesis, the functional interplay between RNA epitranscriptomic modifications and nuclear events in ICC remains poorly elucidated. Here, we identify N-acetyltransferase 10 (NAT10), the writer for N4-acetylcytidine (ac4C) mRNA modification, as a critical regulator of ICC malignancy. Integrated multiomics profiling reveals that NAT10-mediated ac4C modification enhances the mRNA stability of chromatin assembly factor 1 subunit A (CHAF1A), a key chromatin regulator. Mechanistically, the NAT10–ac4C–CHAF1A axis robustly and epigenetically suppresses the expression of the nuclear transposable element HERV9NC, leading to diminished double-stranded RNA accumulation. This epigenetic silencing not only fuels ICC proliferation and migration but also attenuates intrinsic innate immune responses, thereby fostering an immunosuppressive tumor microenvironment characterized by reduced cytotoxic T cell infiltration and impaired tumor surveillance. Pharmacological inhibition of NAT10 with small-molecule compounds demonstrated robust therapeutic efficacy in both patient-derived xenograft and orthotopic tumor transplantation models of ICC. Collectively, our study unveils NAT10 as a master integrator of RNA epitranscriptomic reprogramming and nuclear chromatin dynamics in ICC, providing interesting mechanistic insights into the molecular basis of ICC progression. We also establish NAT10-ac4C modification as a druggable vulnerability, offering a promising therapeutic strategy for this aggressive malignancy.
discussion: DiscussionDuring malignant progression, transcriptomic alterations are coordinated across multiple levels, with epigenetic and posttranscriptional mechanisms remaining underexplored relative to transcriptional regulation. Beyond DNA and histone modifications, mRNA modifications have emerged as a critical regulatory layer in tumorigenesis. While m6A methylation has been extensively studied in oncogenesis , the role of mRNA acetylation in ICC progression remains poorly understood. NAT10, the sole writer of ac4C, enhances mRNA stability and translational efficiency when acetylation occurs in coding sequences . Dysregulated ac4C and NAT10 contribute to multiple malignancies, including myeloma, bladder cancer, colorectal carcinoma, hepatocellular carcinoma, and acute leukemia . For instance, NAT10 modulates myeloma cell proliferation and apoptosis . drives chemoresistance in bladder cancer , and promotes proliferation and metastasis in colorectal carcinoma . However, the functional role of NAT10 and its downstream targets in ICC pathogenesis remains elusive.In this study, we employed a panel of ICC cell lines and a CDX model to systematically delineate the functional role of the NAT10–ac4C axis in ICC pathogenesis. NAT10 ablation markedly suppressed ICC cell proliferation, migration, and tumorigenicity. Integrative multiomics analyses identified CHAF1A, a chromatin-associated epigenetic regulator, as a direct mechanistic substrate of NAT10. We further demonstrated that NAT10 mediated ac4C modification on CHAF1A transcripts, with CHAF1A functionally recapitulating the oncogenic effects of NAT10, as validated by rescue experiments. Moreover, combined RNA-seq, acRIP-seq, CUT&Tag, and ATAC-seq revealed that the NAT10–ac4C–CHAF1A axis governs intergenic chromatin remodeling. Interestingly, over 55% of accessible regions mapped to transposable elements, predominantly HERV9NC-int in LTR/ERV family. The opening of these TE-derived regulatory elements may facilitate the activation of inflammatory signaling via dsRNA intermediates, thereby potentially promoting ICC progression. This epigenetic modulation effectively suppressed the generation of dsRNA, thereby promoting tumor proliferation and migrative potential while concurrently attenuating intracellular innate immune responses. Consequently, the diminished dsRNA sensing led to reduced T cell infiltration, impairing antitumor immunity and facilitating immune evasion. An interesting observation in our study is that knockdown of either MDA5 or RIG-I partially rescued the antitumor phenotype caused by CHAF1A depletion, despite the fact that these two sensors are classically thought to recognize distinct dsRNA species. One possible explanation is that derepression of HERV9NC upon perturbation of the NAT10–CHAF1A axis generates a heterogeneous pool of endogenous dsRNAs. Transposable elements, particularly ERVs, are known to produce dsRNA species through bidirectional transcription and inverted-repeat-derived hairpin structures, thereby generating RNAs with diverse lengths and conformations. Such heterogeneity may allow simultaneous engagement of both MDA5 and RIG-I, as has been reported for ERV-derived dsRNAs in other settings . Our findings therefore suggest that HERV9NC-derived dsRNA does not activate a single sensor in isolation, but rather triggers a broader endogenous RNA-sensing network involving both MDA5- and RIG-I-dependent signaling.Despite the remarkable success of immune checkpoint inhibitors (ICIs) treatment in other solid tumors, current limited clinical evidence indicates their therapeutic benefits remain modest in ICC. This treatment resistance primarily derives from the characteristic Immunosuppressive tumor microenvironment of ICC. To overcome the current resistance predicament of immunotherapy in ICC, combination strategies have emerged as a mainstream approach. Our findings in this study suggest that targeting NAT10 in ICC may represent a promising therapeutic paradigm capable of simultaneously disrupting tumor cell-intrinsic resistance and TME immunosuppression.Overall, our investigations yielded interesting insights into the molecular pathogenesis of ICC, representing significant conceptual advances in understanding this malignancy (SI Appendix, Fig. S8). The study mechanistically elucidated the NAT10–ac4C–CHAF1A regulatory axis, thereby establishing a promising therapeutic paradigm for targeted intervention in ICC progression.
figure_legends: NAT10 is a key unfavorable prognostic factor of ICC. (A) Prognostic analysis of RNA modification-related genes at protein (Left) and transcriptomic (Right) levels in ICC. The Venn diagram (Lower Left) illustrates the intersection of RNA modification genes significantly correlated with poor clinical outcomes (P < 0.05) at both translational and transcriptional levels. (B) Immunohistochemical staining of NAT10 expression in a multicase ICC tissue microarray. Right: adjacent normal tissues (Normal); Left: tumor tissues (Tumor). (C) Violin plots with boxplots showed significantly higher NAT10 protein and mRNA expression in tumor tissues compared to adjacent normal tissues across multiple datasets. P values were determined by Wilcoxon test. (D) Kaplan–Meier survival plots showing high expression of NAT10 at both protein and mRNA levels in tumor tissues consistently correlates with poor prognosis across multiple datasets. P values were determined by Log-rank test. (E) Dot blot analysis of ac4C modification levels in normal cholangiocyte cell line (HIBEC) and three ICC cell lines (RBE, CCLP1, and HuCCT-1). (F) Western blot analysis of NAT10 expression in the indicated cell lines. The Lower panel shows quantification of NAT10 protein levels normalized to loading control. (Scale bar, 50 μm.) Data represent mean ± SD of at least three independent experiments (*P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, two-tailed Student t test).

NAT10 knockdown inhibits malignant behaviors in ICC cells. (A) RT-qPCR analysis confirming efficient NAT10 knockdown in RBE cells transduced with sh-Ctrl or two independent shRNAs targeting NAT10 (sh-NAT10#1 and sh-NAT10#2). (B) Western blot analyses demonstrating reduced NAT10 protein abundance after NAT10 silencing (Left). Quantification of NAT10 protein levels normalized to the loading control (Right). (C) Dot blot analyses demonstrating reduced global RNA ac4C modification levels upon NAT10 silencing. (D) CCK-8 assays monitoring proliferation over the indicated time course. (E) Representative images (Left) and quantification (Right) of colony-formation assays. (F) EdU assays assessing DNA synthesis. The percentage of EdU-positive cells was quantified in the Right panel. (G) Transwell assays comparing the migrative capacity of sh-Ctrl and sh-NAT10 cells. (H) Gross appearance of subcutaneous xenografts generated from sh-Ctrl and sh-NAT10 HuCCT-1 cells in nude mice. (I and J) Quantitative analysis of xenograft volume (I) and weight (J). (K and L) Representative in vivo bioluminescence images (K) and corresponding quantitative analysis of mean fluorescence intensity (L) for orthotopic ICC tumors from control and Nat10-knockdown groups. (Scale bar, 50 μm.) Data represent mean ± SD of at least three independent experiments (*P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001, ****P < 0.0001, two-tailed Student t test).

NAT10 knockdown significantly reduces ac4C abundance and disrupts its distribution in ICC cells. (A) Poly(A) RNAs were digested to mononucleosides and analyzed by UPLC-MS/MS in sh-Ctrl and sh-NAT10 cells. (B) Numbers of ac4C peaks were detected in sh-Ctrl and sh-NAT10 cells. (C) Venn plot showing intersection of the ac4C peaks in sh-Ctrl and sh-NAT10 ICC cells. (D) Genomic distribution of ac4C peaks of sh-Ctrl and sh-NAT10 ICC cells. (E) Distributions of the ac4C peaks across transcripts. (F) Most significant motifs in sh-Ctrl and sh-NAT10 ac4C peaks. (G) Volcano plot of differential ac4C peaks between sh-Ctrl and sh-NAT10 ICC cells. Data represent mean ± SD of at least three independent experiments (**P < 0.01, two-tailed Student t test).

Chromatin-remodeling factor CHAF1A is a key NAT10 downstream target in ICC. (A) Volcano plot of differentially expressed proteins between sh-Ctrl and sh-NAT10 ICC cells. (B) Bar plot showing the enriched GO terms of downregulated proteins in sh-NAT10 cells. (C) Scatter plot of fold changes between differentially expressed genes and proteins in sh-Ctrl vs. sh-NAT10 cells. The red dashed line represents the fitted curve. (D) Venn diagram showing the intersection of downregulated proteins, downregulated genes, and genes with downregulated ac4C peaks. (E) Heatmaps of protein expression and fold changes in proteomic, acRIP-seq and transcriptomic data for seven overlapped genes. (F) RT-qPCR analysis of NAT10’s potential targets in RBE and HuCCT-1 cells transduced with sh-Ctrl and sh-NAT10. (G) IGV browser visualization of ac4C peaks in CHAF1A and representation of ac4C motif within gene. (H) ac4C-RIP-qPCR showing reduced ac4C enrichment on CHAF1A transcripts in sh-NAT10 vs. sh-Ctrl cells. (I) Decay curves for CHAF1A in sh-Ctrl and sh-NAT10 RBE cells. (J) Western blot analysis (Left) and quantification (Right) of CHAF1A protein expression after NAT10 silencing. (K) RT-qPCR analysis of CHAF1A mRNA expression in RBE cells with NAT10 overexpression. (L) Western blot analysis (Upper) and quantification (Lower Left) of CHAF1A protein expression in RBE cells with NAT10 overexpression. (M) Decay curves for CHAF1A in OE-Vech and OE-NAT10 RBE cells. (N) RT-qPCR analysis of CHAF1A expression in sh-Ctrl or sh-NAT10 RBE cells cotransfected with empty vector (OE-Vech) or CHAF1A overexpression construct (OE-CHAF1A). (O) CCK-8 assays showing rescue of proliferation in sh-NAT10 cells upon CHAF1A reexpression. (P) Transwell assays (Left) and corresponding quantification (Right) for the indicated groups. (Q) Representative colony-formation (Left) and quantitative analysis (Right). (Scale bar, 50 μm.) Data represent mean ± SD of at least three independent experiments (*P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001, ****P < 0.0001, two-tailed Student t test).

NAT10–ac4C–CHAF1A axis inhibits the expression of transposable elements. (A) Volcano plot of differentially expressed genes between si-Ctrl and si-CHAF1A ICC cells. (B) Volcano plot of differential ATAC peaks between si-Ctrl and si-CHAF1A cells. (C) Overlapped upregulated ATAC peaks in si-CHAF1A and sh-NAT10 cells (Top), the genomic distribution of these overlapped ATAC peaks (Bottom). (D) Volcano plot of differentially expressed TEs between si-Ctrl and si-CHAF1A cells. (E) Bar plot showing the counts of downregulated TE families in si-CHAF1A cells. (F) Expression heatmaps of sense and antisense TEs in si-Ctrl and si-CHAF1A cells. (G) Gene sets enrichment analysis (GSEA) of innate immune related hallmarks in si-CHAF1A vs. si-Ctrl cells.

CHAF1A promotes ICC malignancy by suppressing HERV9NC-derived dsRNA accumulation. (A) Immunofluorescence staining of cytosolic dsRNA in RBE cells transfected with si-Ctrl and si-CHAF1A. (Scale bar, 25 μm.) (B) Quantification of intracellular dsRNA by ELISA. (C) RT-qPCR analysis of proinflammatory factors and dsRNA sensors related genes in sh-Ctrl and sh-NAT10 ICC cells. (D and E) Immunofluorescence (D) and ELISA assay (E) assessing dsRNA levels in sh-NAT10 cells transduced with empty vector (OE-Vech) or CHAF1A overexpression construct (OE-CHAF1A). (Scale bar, 25 μm.) (F) Bar plot of log2 fold changes for dsRNA-related TEs in si-CHAF1A vs. si-Ctrl cells. (G) Peak heatmaps displaying ATAC-seq (si-CHAF1A vs. si-Ctrl and sh-NAT10 vs. sh-Ctrl) and CUT&Tag of CHAF1A (si-CHAF1A vs. si-Ctrl) signals within 4-kb window around the genomic loci of HERV9NC-int. (H) IGV browser tracks of representative NAT10–ac4C–CHAF1A downstream HERV9NC’s sense (Top) and antisense (Bottom) loci of showing indicated chromatin features. (I) J2-RIP-qPCR showing enhanced binding of HERV9NC transcripts to the dsRNA-specific J2 antibody in sh-NAT10 vs. sh-Ctrl RBE cells. (J) RT-qPCR analysis of HERV9NC expression in sh-Ctrl and sh-NAT10 RBE cells cotransfected with si-Ctrl and si-HERV9NC. (K) CCK-8 assays measuring cell proliferation over the indicated time course. (L) Transwell assay showing evaluating migrative capacity of sh-NAT10 cells after HERV9NC knock-down. (Scale bar, 50 μm.) The Lower panel presents quantitative analysis. (M) ELISA quantification of intracellular dsRNA in the indicated groups. Data represent mean ± SD of at least three independent experiments (*P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001, ****P < 0.0001, two-tailed Student t test).

Single-cell resolution dissection reveals an immunosuppressive microenvironment in NAT10-high ICC. (A) UMAP visualization of major cell types in 25 ICC tumor specimens. (B) Bubble plot showing the NAT10 expression in major cell types. (C) Violin plots showing NAT10 expression in malignant epithelial cells with high vs. low NAT10 expression. (D) Comparative analysis of major cell type proportions in NAT10-high vs. NAT10-low tumors. (E) UMAP visualization of all NK/T cell subtypes. (F) Ro/e analysis of NK/T cell subsets in NAT10-high vs. NAT10-low groups. (G) GSEA of innate immune related hallmarks in NAT10-high vs. NAT10-low tumor epithelial cells. (H) CellChat analysis depicting the interaction quantity and strength between malignant epithelial cells and T cell subsets in NAT10-high vs. NAT10-low groups. (I) Diminished receptor–ligand interaction strength in NAT10-high compared to NAT10-low depicted by bubble plot. (J) Schematic of the coculture system between LD1 cells (with or without Nat10-knockdown) and murine CD8+ T cells. (K) Flow cytometry analysis of the proportion Granzyme B positive CD8+ T cells following coculture with control or Nat10-knockdown LD1 cells. (L) Schematic of flow cytometric analysis of Granzyme B positive CD8+ T cell infiltration in orthotopic ICC tumors derived from control and Nat10-knockdown groups. (M) The proportion of Granzyme B positive CD8+ T cells within orthotopic ICC tumors from control and Nat10-knockdown groups was analyzed by flow cytometry. Data represent mean ± SD of at least three independent experiments (*P < 0.05, ****P < 0.0001, two-tailed Student t test).

The NAT10 inhibitors demonstrate therapeutic efficacy in ICC. (A) Dot blot analysis of ac4C RNA levels in ICC cells treated with increasing concentrations of NAT10 inhibitor Remodelin. (B) CCK-8 assays showing the dose-dependent cytotoxicity of Remodelin in the ICC cell lines. (C) ELISA quantification of intracellular dsRNA in the control and Remodelin-treated groups. (D and E) Colony-formation (D) and transwell assays (E) illustrating the concentration-dependent suppression of clonogenic growth and migrative capacity by Remodelin. (F) Gross appearance of spontaneous ICC tumors following treatment with Remodelin or Panobinostat. (G and H) Quantitative analysis of tumor volume (G) and weight (H) for the groups depicted in (F). (I and J) Representative in vivo bioluminescence images (I) and corresponding quantitative analysis of total flux (J) for orthotopic ICC tumors following treatment with vehicle control, Remodelin, or Panobinostat. (K) Kaplan–Meier survival curves of orthotopic ICC tumor-bearing mice treated with vehicle control, Remodelin, or Panobinostat. P values were calculated by the Log-rank test. (L) Quantitative analysis of tumor-infiltrating immune cell populations in orthotopic ICC tumors from mice treated with vehicle control, Remodelin, or Panobinostat, as determined by flow cytometry. (M) Representative Immunofluorescence images showing CD8+ T cells (green) in tumors from control vs. Remodelin- and Panobinostat-treated groups. Increased CD8+ T cell infiltration is evident in the treatment group. Nuclei are counterstained with DAPI (blue). (Scale bar, 50 μm.) Data represent mean ± SD of at least three independent experiments (*P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001, ****P < 0.0001, two-tailed Student t test).
